heading: Primary care assessment
html:
History

  • Claudication: calf, thigh, or buttock pain brought on by walking and relieved by rest within minutes
  • Rest pain: constant foot or toe pain, worse at night, often relieved by hanging the leg dependent
  • Wound healing: any non-healing wound below the knee lasting more than 2 weeks
  • Risk factors: smoking, diabetes, hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia, CKD, cardiovascular history
  • Functional impact: walking distance, quality of life

Examination

  • Colour and temperature of feet
  • Peripheral pulses: femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial
  • Capillary refill time
  • Trophic changes: skin atrophy, hair loss, dystrophic nails, ulceration, gangrene
  • Abdominal palpation for aneurysm

ABPI measurement

  • Normal: 0.9–1.3
  • Claudication: typically 0.5–0.9 — refer if <0.9 (D&G threshold)
  • CLTI: typically <0.5 — urgent referral
  • >1.3 (calcified arteries): unreliable — particularly in diabetes, end-stage renal disease, elderly; refer if symptomatic

Investigations

  • FBC, U&E, lipid profile, HbA1c, fasting glucose
  • ECG (cardiovascular risk)

Red flags — act immediately

  • Acute limb ischaemia: sudden severe limb pain, pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesia, paralysis, cold limb → 999 / Emergency Department
  • Severe infection/sepsis with systemic signs → discuss urgently with Hairmyres on-call vascular

heading: Primary care management
html:
Manage all patients with intermittent claudication in primary care first. Refer only if first-line treatment fails or referral criteria are met.

Lifestyle and risk factor modification

  • Smoking cessation: strongest intervention — advice and pharmacotherapy (varenicline, NRT)
  • Supervised exercise programme: 2 hours/week for minimum 3 months before referral (NICE NG237)
  • Encourage walking to the point of claudication and through the pain
  • Weight reduction
  • Blood pressure control (BHS guidelines)
  • Glycaemic optimisation — especially important in diabetic patients

Antiplatelet therapy

  • Clopidogrel 75mg daily (preferred in PAD)

Lipid-lowering therapy

  • Atorvastatin 80mg daily for patients with PAD and total cholesterol >3.5 mmol/L

Vasoactive therapy

  • Consider naftidrofuryl oxalate 100mg three times daily, increasing to 200mg three times daily after 3 months if tolerated; stop if no benefit after a further 3 months (NICE TA223)

Foot care

  • Inspect feet regularly — especially in diabetic patients
  • Refer for structured diabetic foot care if ulceration, deformity, or sensory neuropathy present

heading: Who to refer
html:
Hospital admission (same day)

Discuss urgently with Hairmyres on-call vascular reg/cons, or send to Emergency Department:

  • Acute limb ischaemia: sudden pain, pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesia, paralysis, cold limb
  • Clinical evidence of severe infection/sepsis with systemic signs

Urgent (non-cancer outpatient)

  • Rest pain: constant foot or toe pain, typically worse at night, relieved by hanging the leg dependent
  • Non-healing wound more than 2 weeks duration below the knee
  • Gangrene on the foot

Routine (outpatient)

  • Quality of life severely affected by claudication, or claudication distance less than 40 metres
  • ABPI less than 0.9
  • ABPI greater than 1.4 in diabetic, end-stage renal disease, or extremely elderly patients — result is unreliable; refer if symptomatic
  • Failed first-line treatment (supervised exercise, risk factor modification, naftidrofuryl)

Before referring: consider whether the patient would be suitable for vascular intervention. If unsure, email the vascular service first: dg.vascularservice@nhs.scot

Include in referral

  • ABPI result with date
  • Baseline bloods: lipids, HbA1c, FBC, U&E
  • Current medications (especially antiplatelet, anticoagulant, statin)
  • Cardiovascular history and comorbidities
  • Smoking status

heading: Who not to refer
html:
• Mild or moderate claudication with ABPI 0.9–1.3, good quality of life, and no red flag features — manage in primary care with exercise and risk factor modification
  • Until first-line treatment has been tried for at least 3 months: supervised exercise, smoking cessation, risk factor optimisation, clopidogrel, high-intensity statin, naftidrofuryl if tolerated
  • Very frail patients or those with significant comorbidities where vascular intervention would not be appropriate — discuss informally with the vascular team before referring: dg.vascularservice@nhs.scot
  • Venous leg ulceration — not an arterial vascular referral; ensure ABPI ≥0.8 before applying compression bandaging

heading: References
html:
NHS Scotland / Local

  • NHS D&G Vascular Service: Arterial Insufficiency Referral Criteria (local guideline)
  • SIGN Guideline 89: Diagnosis and Management of Peripheral Arterial Disease

NICE & CKS

  • NICE NG237: Peripheral Arterial Disease: Diagnosis and Management (2023)
  • NICE TA223: Naftidrofuryl Oxalate for Intermittent Claudication (2011)
  • NICE CKS: Peripheral Arterial Disease
